BFS211 · Hear Me Talking To You · Week 07

The readings, week by week

What each reading is trying to say, in plain language, with a link to read it. The summaries stay close to each author's own argument. Where the full text was on hand, a short "From the reading" note draws on the article itself.

Week 1

The Resistant Black Tradition

Olly Wilson, “Black Music as an Art Form” Black Music Research Journal, vol. 3, 1983, pp. 1-22.

What it says

Wilson defines Black music as a broad creative approach shaped by African and African American musical values. He treats sound, structure, function, and cultural setting as signs that Black music has its own art logic.

Read the full text

Samuel A. Floyd Jr., “Ring Shout! Literary Studies, Historical Studies, and Black Music Inquiry” Black Music Research Journal, vol. 11, no. 2, 1991, pp. 265-287.

What it says

Floyd argues that the African ring shout is a usable tool for tracing how musical, gestural, and verbal practices carry continuity across Black traditions, from spirituals through to popular forms, without flattening their differences.

Read the full text

Cheryl L. Keyes, “Sound, Voice, and Spirit: Teaching in the Black Music Vernacular” Black Music Research Journal, vol. 29, no. 1, 2009, pp. 11-24.

What it says

Keyes outlines a way of teaching the Black music vernacular through sound, voice, and spirit as analytical concepts rather than decorative language. She draws on African American oral and performance traditions.

Read the full text

Portia K. Maultsby, “Black Spirituals: An Analysis of Textual Forms and Structures” The Black Perspective in Music, vol. 4, no. 1, 1976, pp. 54-69. (Assigned in Week 2; grouped here with the tradition.)

What it says

Maultsby analyzes the textual forms and structures of Black spirituals. She catalogs the patterns of call-and-response, repetition, and theological content, and shows how spirituals carried both worship and resistance.

From the reading

In the article itself, Maultsby opens by arguing that you can only understand a kind of music by studying its origin, its social development, and its musical features together. She then works through the spirituals one form at a time, looking closely at how the words are built and repeated.

Read the full textFull PDF in the course Readings (Blackboard)

Week 2

Racial Terror and Witness

The Week 2 reading, Maultsby on Black spirituals, is grouped with the tradition in Week 1, where it fits. Week 2 itself centres on Billie Holiday’s “Strange Fruit” as listening.

Week 3

Nina Simone and Live Embodiment

Jasmine Mena and P. Khalil Saucier, “Don't Let Me Be Misunderstood: Nina Simone's Africana Womanism” Journal of Black Studies, vol. 45, no. 3, 2014, pp. 247-265.

What it says

Mena and Saucier use Africana womanism to read a large part of Nina Simone's catalogue. They argue Simone's lyrics raised political consciousness while also naming Black women's emotional, psychological, and communal life.

Read the full text

Dorothy Randall Tsuruta, “I Ain't about to be Non-Violent, Honey” The Black Scholar, vol. 29, no. 2-3, 1999, pp. 54-58.

What it says

Tsuruta reads Simone as a Black woman artist who refused the idea that she had to be quiet or respectable to be heard. The article focuses on Simone's language, her political stance, and her direct naming of violence.

Read the full text

Week 4

Civil Rights and Resistance, Part I

Bernice Johnson Reagon, “Let the Church Sing “Freedom”” Black Music Research Journal, vol. 7, 1987, pp. 105-118. (Also assigned in Week 5.)

What it says

Reagon explains how Black church music, freedom singing, and collective movement practice are connected, and how freedom songs worked as a shared movement voice.

From the reading

Reagon opens by placing the freedom songs inside the 1955 to 1965 movement, when mass civil disobedience served notice on the nation and the world. She shows how the song of the Black church became the collective voice of that protest.

Read the full textFull PDF in the course Readings (Blackboard)

B.G. Rhule, “A Change Is Gonna Come, Sam Cooke (1964)” National Recording Registry essay, Library of Congress.

What it says

Read for Sam Cooke, the Civil Rights-era meaning of the song, and why the recording matters.

From the reading

Rhule's essay tells the story behind the song. Cooke wrote it after hearing Bob Dylan's “Blowin' in the Wind” and feeling that he, as a Black man, should write such a song. Rhule ties the song's meaning to Cooke's own life under Jim Crow on the touring circuit, to his sense of dignity, and to his pioneering work as a Black record-label and publishing owner.

Read the full textFull PDF in the course Readings (Blackboard)

Gregory Alan Barnes, ““I Have a Dream” Speech by Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr. (August 28, 1963)” National Recording Registry essay, Library of Congress.

What it says

Read for historical context around the speech and the March on Washington.

Read the full text

Week 5

Soul and After (Part II)

Portia K. Maultsby, “Soul Music: Its Sociological and Political Significance in American Popular Culture” The Journal of Popular Culture, vol. 17, no. 2, 1983, pp. 51-60.

What it says

Maultsby argues that soul music in the late 1960s and 1970s served as a vehicle for Black self-awareness, political protest, and cultural assertion.

Read the full text

DJ Lynnée Denise, “The Afterlife of Aretha Franklin's Rock Steady: A Case Study in DJ Scholarship” The Black Scholar, vol. 49, no. 3, 2019, pp. 62-72.

What it says

Denise applies Black feminist DJ scholarship to Aretha Franklin's Rock Steady, reading sampling, memory, and listening practice as Black women's musical thought.

Read the full text

Bernice Johnson Reagon, “Let the Church Sing “Freedom”” Black Music Research Journal, vol. 7, 1987, pp. 105-118. (Same article as Week 4; revisited here.)

What it says

Revisited this week to hear how Sweet Honey in the Rock carries memory, collective voice, and Ella Baker's organising tradition.

Read the full textFull PDF in the course Readings (Blackboard)

Week 6

Ethiopianism and New-World African Identity

Randy R. Goldson, “Liberating the Mind: Rastafari and the Theorization of Maroonage as Epistemological (Dis)engagement” Journal of Black Studies, vol. 51, no. 4, 2020, pp. 368-385.

What it says

Goldson explains Rastafari as a way of thinking and knowing outside Babylon's system. Use this reading for Ethiopianism, Babylon, King, Zion, and liberation as a way of knowing.

Read the full text

David W. Stowe, “Babylon Revisited: Psalm 137 as American Protest Song” Black Music Research Journal, vol. 32, no. 1, 2012, pp. 95-112.

What it says

Stowe traces Psalm 137 as a protest song that travels. Use this reading for Babylon, exile, captivity, return, and Black musical adaptation across the diaspora.

From the reading

Stowe starts with the 1973 film The Harder They Come and the Melodians' “Rivers of Babylon,” which reworks Psalm 137. He follows that exile psalm, the song of Babylon and return, as it travels through reggae and across the Black Atlantic into protest.

Read the full textFull PDF in the course Readings (Blackboard)
